---
title: "Prevalence of food allergies in South Asia"
author: "Shweta R. Arakali, Todd D. Green, Chitra Dinakar"
year: 2017
doi: "10.1016/j.anai.2016.09.441"
journal: "Annals of Allergy, Asthma & Immunology"
volume: "118:16–20"
pmid: "27864090"
access: "Paywalled — ScienceDirect. PDF at annallergy.org failed to fetch."
source_quality: "Abstract + search result summaries. Full text not accessed. PDF fetch failed."
---

# Prevalence of food allergies in South Asia

**Authors:** Shweta R. Arakali, Todd D. Green, Chitra Dinakar
**Year:** 2017 (published online Nov 2016)
**DOI:** 10.1016/j.anai.2016.09.441
**Journal:** Annals of Allergy, Asthma & Immunology, 118:16–20
**PMID:** 27864090

## Why this paper

Described as a foundational review identifying knowledge gaps in native Asian Indian populations. Published 2017, meaning it predates most other papers in our set — useful as a baseline for what was known before the more recent synthesis work.

## Extracted points

> **Source quality note:** Points from PubMed abstract and search result summaries. Full text not accessed.

1. Study methodology: PubMed search using "India and food allergy," "Asia and food allergy," "South Asia and food allergy." South Asia defined as India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka. — [ABSTRACT/SEARCH SUMMARY]
   Initial pool: 169 articles. Reviewed in detail: 47. Primary focus on: 10 studies (case reports, surveys, prospective and cross-sectional studies).
   For us: Of 169 articles, only 10 met inclusion criteria — this is the quantitative measure of how sparse the evidence base was as of 2016.

2. Main conclusion: "a variety of unusual and unique allergens and an overall low incidence of food allergy" in South Asia. — [ABSTRACT — likely close to direct quote]
   For us: "Unusual and unique" from a Western reference point — these allergens are not on the Big 8/9 or Big 14 lists. This is a direct argument for why India needs its own list.

3. Most of India's population consumes "a largely but not solely vegetarian diet" consisting mainly of rice, legumes, grains, and vegetables. Spices that are staples: red chili, coriander, cumin, mustard seed, fenugreek, ginger, turmeric, curry leaves. — [SEARCH SUMMARY]
   For us: Dietary context is critical. These staples are also potential allergens — mustard is covered by the EU Big 14 (as mustard), but none of the others are on major regulatory lists.

4. Allergens for which sensitisation was confirmed in India (from search summary): chickpea, eggplant, walnut, coconut, prawn, mango, and betel leaf. — [SEARCH SUMMARY — list may be incomplete or partially inaccurate; needs verification against full paper]
   For us: This is the most specific allergen list we have from the paper. Note: betel leaf (*Piper betle*) is used in paan, a post-meal digestive preparation widely consumed across India — it is not on any major international allergen list.

5. Association between increased food allergy prevalence and individuals living in metropolitan regions or those who have migrated to Westernised communities. — [ABSTRACT/SEARCH SUMMARY]
   For us: Supports the urbanisation-allergy hypothesis from P08.

## Gaps and challenges the authors document

From abstract/search summary:
- Knowledge gap in native Asian Indian populations explicitly identified as the study's motivation
- Only 10 studies out of 169 articles met inclusion criteria — the evidence base as of 2016 was thin

## Questions for full text access

- What were the 10 primary studies? (Knowing which studies formed the evidence base as of 2016 tells us what the subsequent papers in our set built on.)
- Were the confirmed sensitisation allergens (chickpea, eggplant, etc.) from clinical data, skin prick tests, or serum IgE?
- Does the paper comment on methodological standardisation issues?
